generations.text:
My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you this evening with a heart heavy as the storm that now churns beyond the cliffs, and with hands that tremble not from the wind, but from the weight of years unspoken.

Five years have passed since we last exchanged a word, and yet it feels like a lifetime. I have often wondered if you still remember me—what I looked like, how I spoke, or if you even recall that I was ever your father. I have tried, in the quiet hours of the night, to imagine what you might be like now. Have you grown tall? Have you found your own way in the world? Or have you, like me, been shaped by the tides of fate?

The storm is upon us, Eleanor. The sky is black as ink, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have lit the lantern, as I have done every night since the day you left. The light has been a beacon for countless ships, but tonight, I fear it may be the last time I can keep it burning.

I have no regrets, not truly. I have done what I thought was right, and I have lived as I believed a man should—duty-bound, steadfast, and above all, honest. But I have come to see that duty alone does not fill a man’s soul. I have been alone, Eleanor. Not just in this lighthouse, but in my heart. And I have missed you more

**April 12th, 1893**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee at my back, and the sea roiling as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lamps have been lit, the windows sealed, and the fire in the hearth is barely more than a flicker. Yet I sit here, pen in hand, with the weight of five years pressing upon me like the storm that now brews on the horizon.

You were not the one who left, Eliza. I know that now. I have spent these years not in bitterness, but in quiet sorrow—sorrow for the girl I once knew, the daughter I loved, and the woman who walked out on a stormy night, her heart heavier than the sea. I have not written to you, not once, for fear that the words would not carry, or worse, that they would be met with silence.

But tonight—tonight, the storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see another dawn. I have lived my life in service to this light, guiding the lost and the weary, and now I find myself lost in the very thing I have always been meant to protect. I have no one to call for help but the wind, and no one to share my last breath but the sea.

I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls. The sea roars in fury, and the sky is a canvas of black and silver, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear I may not see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing day. I have tried, my child, I have tried to forget, to move on. But the sea is not so cruel as to let a man forget the daughter he once held close. I dream of you still, in the quiet hours when the light burns low and the waves whisper secrets only the wind can carry.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I do not know if this letter will ever reach you. But tonight is different. Tonight, the storm is not just a threat to the lighthouse—it is a threat to my soul. I have always been the keeper of the light, the guardian of those who sail the dark waters. But I fear I have failed you, Eleanor. I fear I have become a shadow of the man I once was.

You were my light, my hope, my reason for standing against the tempests of life. And I let you go. I let you walk away, and I never once reached for you. I told myself it was for your own good, that you would find a better life,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though time has worn away the edges of our quarrel, it has not softened the ache of your absence. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and I have seen countless storms pass over these cliffs, but none have ever felt as cruel or as lonely as the silence between us.

The sea is restless tonight. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that sends shivers down my spine. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and though the light may guide the ships away from the rocks, I fear it may not be enough to guide you back into my life.

I do not know if you will ever forgive me, Eliza. I know I have been a fool, and I know I have failed you in ways that words cannot convey. I have spent these years in regret, watching the horizon from this lonely tower, wondering if I would ever see your face again. I have tried to be a better man, but I fear I have not been the father you deserved.

You were my light, my greatest joy, and I let it go. I let it go because I was afraid, and because I did not know how to mend what had been broken. I was not the man I wanted to be, and

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that beats with a heavy, uncertain rhythm. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs upon which I stand. I have not seen you in five years, Eleanor, and I do not know if I shall live to see another dawn. But if I do, I wish to speak to you once more—before the storm claims me, before the silence between us grows too deep to mend.

I have not written to you in years, and I do not know if these words will reach you. I have no means of sending them beyond the lighthouse, and the sea is restless tonight. Yet I must try, for I fear that if I do not, I may never speak to you again.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child who was too proud to cry, too stubborn to beg. I remember the day you walked out of this house, your small shoulders squared, your eyes full of fire and fury. You said you would not be bound by duty, by obligation, by the life I had carved for you. You said you wanted freedom, and I let you go. I did not know then that it would be the last time I would see you.

I have lived these years alone, Eleanor. The lighthouse has been my only companion, the sea

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the great lamp, its light flickering like a soul in distress, and I find myself compelled to write. Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and yet the weight of that silence feels heavier with each passing day. I have not spoken to you, not truly, and I fear that I may never again. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I feel the need to reach out—not for absolution, but for the sake of my own peace.

The sea has been restless this week, and the wind has grown a voice of its own, moaning through the stones of the cliff like a mournful wail. The lighthouse has stood for generations, but I have felt it tremble beneath me. The lamps have been kept burning, as they always have been, but I wonder if they will hold against the fury that is coming.

I have not seen the sky for days, buried beneath clouds that hang low and heavy, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. And yet, I have not cried. Not since the day you left. You were a child then, barely more than a girl, and I thought I could shield you from the world’s cruel winds. But you chose your own path, and I, in my pride and fear, did not follow.

I have been alone here, you know. The sea is a faithful companion, but it is not a

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea howls outside like a beast in torment, and the wind rattles the windows as if it would tear this old lighthouse down. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of my silence more than ever.

I know you do not want to hear from me. I know you have moved on, that your life has taken a path I could never have followed. You were a child when I last saw you, and in the five years since, I have watched the world change, and with it, my place in it. I have become a man of the sea, a keeper of the light, and a man who has learned to live with the shadows of his past.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the nights you would sit by the hearth, your small hands curled around a book, your eyes wide with wonder. You loved stories, and I loved telling them. I told you of the stars, of the sea, of the ghosts that sail the waves. I told you of the lighthouse, and how it was not just a place, but a promise—that as long as the light burned, so too would hope.

But hope, my dear, is a fragile thing. And I have come to fear that I have let it

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles not from the wind, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last letter, and though the sea may be the same, I fear I have become a stranger to you—perhaps more so than the waves that crash against these rocks.

The lighthouse has stood for over a century, weathering tempests and time alike. Tonight, the storm is coming. The skies are black as ink, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have lit the lamp, as I always have, but I wonder if it will be enough. The light may guide ships, but I fear it may not be enough to guide my heart.

You were once my greatest pride, Eleanor. I remember the day you first stood at the door, wide-eyed and full of questions. You asked me about the stars, and I told you they were the lanterns of the heavens, guiding lost souls just as I guide lost ships. You believed in me, in the light I carried, and in the purpose I had. But time, like the sea, has a way of eroding even the strongest bonds.

I know I have been a poor father, and I regret every moment I let pride or fear keep me from you. I should have come to see you, should have held you in my arms instead of letting distance grow like a weed in the cracks of my heart.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since you left this house, and five years since I last heard your voice. The wind howls through the cliffs like a mournful dirge, and I fear the storm that approaches may take more than just the light from this tower. I am not sure I will survive the night, and if I do not, I hope you will read these words.

You were once the light of my life, Eleanor. The daughter I held in my arms the night you were born, the first smile that warmed my cold heart, the little girl who would sit by the fire and listen to my stories of the sea. I remember the day you left—how you stood at the door, eyes filled with tears, and said, “I need to find my own way.” I did not understand then, and I still do not. But I have tried to forgive you, and I have tried to let go.

I have kept the light burning, just as you once taught me. It is my duty, my purpose. The sea has been cruel to me, as it has been to so many before me. I have seen ships vanish in the night, their crews lost to the dark. But I have also seen the dawn break through, and I have held the light for those who needed it.

Tonight, the sky is dark, and the wind

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. The lantern has been lit, as it always has been, and the light cuts through the darkness like a blade. But tonight, I feel it more than ever—this light, this duty, this burden. It is not just a beacon for the ships, but for my soul, and I fear that soon, I may be the only one left to keep it burning.

Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke. Time has worn the edges of our words, and the silence between us has grown heavy as the storm clouds above. I have not written to you, nor have I expected you to write. Yet, as the wind grows fiercer and the waves crash with a fury I have not known in decades, I find myself thinking of you more than I ever have in all that time.

I know you have your own life, your own path, and I have no right to presume what it may be. But I have come to understand that I was not the father you needed, nor the man you deserved. I was a man of duty, of stone and steel, and I forgot that a heart is not built of iron. I was too busy keeping the light alive to see that the light you needed was not in the lighthouse, but in the home you left behind.

I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roaring as though it would devour the very stones of this cliff. The lighthouse stands firm, but I fear my own bones may not hold long. The storm is upon us, and I am not certain I will see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have counted every hour, every minute, every breath. I have kept the light burning for you, as I once kept the fire in my heart. But now, that fire has dimmed, and the light flickers with a sorrow that no lamp can soothe.

You were a child when you ran from this house, from me, from the life we had built together. I tried to understand, to reach you, but you would not listen. You called me cruel, unfeeling, a man who cared more for the sea than for you. And I suppose, in my own way, I did. For I have always been a man of the sea, of the rocks, of the endless horizon. I have never known how to hold a woman’s hand or comfort a child’s tears. I thought I could do better, but I was wrong.

I have kept this lighthouse not only for the safety of those who sail the waters, but for the hope that one day you might return. That one day, the storm would pass, and you would come back to see the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea roars as if it too has forgotten its place, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and silver. I have kept this lighthouse for over thirty years, and I have never known such a storm. It is as though the heavens themselves are trying to shake me from my post, and I fear they may succeed.

You were not here when I first took this post, nor when I first saw the light shine for the first time on a clear, starry night. You were not here when I met your mother, nor when I watched you take your first steps, nor when I held you in my arms and whispered promises of love and safety. You were not here when I became a man of the sea, and you were not here when I became a man of the light.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not heard your voice in all that time. I have tried to write, but the words would not come. I have tried to call, but the sea would not carry my voice. I have tried to forgive, but the silence between us has grown like a wall, high and unyielding.

I do not know if you still remember me. I do not know if you remember the man who once held you when you were a child, who once read to you by the firelight

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a banshee through the lighthouse, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—not with words, but with the truth that has long been buried in the depths of my soul.

You were once my light, Eliza. Bright, brave, and full of a fire that could not be extinguished. I watched you grow from a child with wide, curious eyes to a woman who left our home with the promise of a brighter future. But that future did not come to you, and I fear it never will. You left, and I did not chase you. I did not beg, nor did I plead. I simply stood at the door, watching you go, and let the silence grow between us.

I have spent these years in the solitude of this lighthouse, tending to the beacon that guides the lost and the weary. It is a duty I have taken upon myself with solemn pride, and in its light, I have found a strange kind of peace. Yet it is not the same as the warmth of your presence. I have not seen your face in years, nor heard your voice, and I have come to fear that I may never again.

Tonight, the storm is unlike

**The Lighthouse, Far Rock Light,  
Isle of Wight,  
October 12th, 1894**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these walls. The sea has grown restless, and the sky is a canvas of ink and flame. I have seen storms before, but never one that feels so… personal. As I stand at the lantern room, the light flickering like a dying ember, I find myself thinking of you—of your laughter, your eyes, and the way you used to chase the waves as a child, not fearing them, but loving them.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left, and five years since I’ve spoken your name without the weight of regret pressing down on my chest. I have tried, I truly have tried, to reach out. I sent letters, I called upon your mother, I even sought the help of the vicar, but you never answered. You never came back.

I know what you must think of me. A man who locked himself away in this lighthouse, who turned his back on the world and the daughter he once held close. But you do not understand the burden I carry. This place is not just a duty—it is a vow. I have sworn to keep the light burning, to guide the lost and the lost at sea. And I have done so, not because I am

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that now rages beyond these walls. Five years have passed since you left, and though the days have dragged on like the slow, grinding turn of the lighthouse beam, I have never once stopped thinking of you. The sea, my dear, is a cruel and fickle mistress, but even it cannot match the coldness of the silence you have left behind.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. Not a word, not a letter, not even a glance from the window when you passed by on your way to the village. You were always so quick to leave, so quick to forget. I used to think I could outrun the pain of your absence, but the years have only made it more bitter. I have become a man of the sea, and the sea has become my only companion.

Tonight, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones ache. I have seen storms like this before, but this one feels different. The sky is black as ink, and the air is thick with the scent of impending ruin. I fear I may not see dawn.

I do not know if you will ever forgive me, Eliza. I do not know if you will ever come back. But if you should, I would not turn you away. I would light the lamp,

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee at my door, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself unable to hold my tongue.

I have always been a man of silence, Eleanor. A man who kept his feelings locked away in the lantern room, where the light burns bright and the world outside is but a blur. But tonight, the light is dim, and the silence is deafening.

I know you think I have forgotten you. That I have turned my back on you. But I have not. I have watched the moon rise and set, and I have watched the seasons pass, all the while wondering if you still remember the day you left. The day you walked out of our house, your mother's hand in yours, and your eyes filled with something I could not name—anger, sorrow, or perhaps a mixture of both.

I have not written to you, not because I do not care, but because I feared what I might say. I feared that my words would not be enough. That they would not mend the rift between us. That they would only deepen the wound.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child who thought she understood the world, and who thought she knew what was best for us. But

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles, not from the cold, but from the weight of what I must say. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside these stone walls, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. Tonight, the storm will test the lighthouse, and I fear it may test me as well. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I shall live to see the morning.

You were a child when you left, as I was a man who thought himself strong enough to bear the burden of your absence. I told myself that time would mend the rift, that the sea would smooth the jagged edges of my heart. But time, like the tides, only brings more waves, and I have grown weary of the storm.

I have watched the horizon for years, waiting for a sign, a letter, a word from you. But the waves have only brought me the echoes of your laughter, the memory of your small hands in mine as you once climbed the steps of this tower. You were my light, Eleanor, my guiding star. And now, I fear I have become a shadow, a mere sentinel of the dark.

I do not know if you still remember the nights we spent together, when the world seemed to pause and the stars would shine so brightly that they hurt our eyes. I do not know if you still remember the stories I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart that aches with the weight of years. It has been five long, sorrowful years since I last set eyes upon you, and now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the cliffs, I find myself once more at the edge of despair—both for the world outside and for the silence that has grown between us.

You were always a spirited child, Eliza, with a fire in your soul that could not be tamed. I tried, I truly did, to guide you with the same steady hand that I used to steer the lighthouse beacon through the darkest nights. But you were never meant for the quiet of this place. You longed for the world beyond the sea, for the bustling streets of London, for a life that did not demand the watchful eye of a father who could not always be there.

And so you left. I watched you go, and though I did not say it then, I feared that you would never return. I have not spoken to you since, and I have not written, for I feared what might be said. But now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that rivals the storm in my own heart, I find that I must try once more.

You were my only child, my light in the darkness. And though I may have failed you in many ways, I have never ceased

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though the years have worn away the edges of our quarrel, they have not softened the pain it left behind. I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you angry. I do not blame you for that.

You were once my greatest joy, my little star in the night sky, and I have spent every night since you left watching for it. The lighthouse has become my only companion, and I have come to know its every creak and groan as well as I know the contours of my own soul. The sea, too, has become a familiar friend, though it has always been a cruel one. Tonight, however, the sea is restless, and I fear it may claim me before I have the chance to speak again.

You were not wrong to leave. I know now that I was not the man you deserved. I was too stubborn, too proud, too blind to see the love you gave me was not enough for me to return. I was a fool to let pride keep me from you, and a worse fool to let it keep me from myself. I have spent these years in silence, not because I was not sorry, but because I did not know how to say it.

The storm is coming. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves are rising

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I write this with trembling hands, my heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken and the storm that now howls at the windows of my lonely tower. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though the sea has not forgotten me, I fear I have forgotten how to be a father.  

The wind grows louder, and the sea, that old and unrelenting companion, roars like a beast in pain. I have kept the light burning all night, as I always have, but tonight it feels as though the very heavens are crying. I have no doubt the storm will take what it will, and I pray it takes nothing of you.  

I know you do not wish to hear from me. You have every right not to. I was a fool, Eleanor, a man who let pride and silence build a wall between us. I was too proud to apologize, too stubborn to reach out. I thought that by keeping my distance, I was protecting you from the burden of my failures. But I was wrong. You were the one who suffered, and I was too blind to see it.  

I have no grand words to offer you now, no eloquent apologies that could undo the years of silence. But if the sea grants me one more dawn, I swear I will come to you. I will find you, not as a man who has lost his way, but as a father who has finally learned

**April 3rd, 1892**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear I may not live to see its arrival. The sea has been restless this evening, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though tonight it feels heavier than ever. The light will guide the ships, as it always has, but I wonder if it will guide *me* home.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left. I have not spoken to you, nor have I heard from you. The silence between us has grown thick as the fog that coils around these rocks. I have tried to forget, but the past is a cruel thing—it lingers, even in the quietest moments. I see your face in the waves, hear your voice in the wind. It haunts me, more than the storm that now threatens to consume us all.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. That is what I told myself when I chose this life. A lighthouse keeper is not just a man with a lamp and a tower—he is a guardian, a sentinel against the chaos of the sea. I have kept that lamp burning for years, through tempests and calm, through the years of your absence. I have never once let it go out. I thought that was my way of holding on to something, something

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken, and a mind troubled by the howling winds that gather outside these stone walls. The sea has been restless all evening, and the sky is a canvas of black and silver, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear this storm may be the end of me, and I find myself compelled to speak, not only to the wind, but to you.

Five years have passed since you left, and though I have kept the lighthouse lit, I have kept my heart in darkness. I have not written, nor have I dared to hope that you might return. The world has moved on, and I have remained here, alone, with the sea as my only companion. Yet, in these quiet hours, when the waves crash against the cliffs and the wind howls like a wounded beast, I find myself thinking of you—of your laughter, your eyes, the way you used to dance in the light of the beacon.

I do not know if you still remember the nights we spent together, when you would come to the lighthouse and sit by the fire, listening to the stories I told of sailors lost to the sea, or of the stars that guide us through the darkest of nights. You were always so curious, so full of life, and I took it for granted, as I often do with those I love. I was blind to the pain

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five years have passed since you left, and though the days have dragged on like the endless nights in this lighthouse, I have never forgotten you. Not a single moment.

You were once my greatest pride, my light in the darkness. Now, I fear I have become a shadow—fading, brittle, and more alone than I ever thought possible.

Tonight, the sea is restless. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost human. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night since the day you left, but tonight feels different. Tonight, I do not know if the light will shine again.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steadfast, that he must stand firm against the storm and guide the lost back to shore. But I have never been so afraid as I am now. I do not know if I will be here when the dawn comes. I do not know if I will be here at all.

I have not written to you before, not because I have no words, but because I feared that even the most honest letter would not be enough. I feared that no words could ever make up for the silence, the absence, the choices I made that led to our parting.

I have not been a good father, Eliza. I have not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea, that old adversary, roars with a fury I have not heard in many a moon. I fear the night may claim me before dawn, and if it does, I hope you will find this letter in the morning light, nestled beside the lamp that has guided ships through storm and calm for over thirty years.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have passed since I last heard your voice. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, though I have whispered it in prayer, as though the very air might carry it to you. I have tried to forgive you, Eleanor, though I do not believe I have truly done so. You left us—your mother and me—like a shadow slipping through the cracks of a storm-torn window, leaving only silence in your wake.

I know you must have thought me cruel, perhaps even unkind. I was not the man I once was. The sea has taken much from me, and the loneliness of this place has worn me down like the salt air. I was not the father you needed, and I was not the man you deserved. I have not been worthy of your love, and I fear I may never be again.

But I write to you now not only as a father, but as a man

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink of a man whose hands have trembled more in the past fortnight than they ever have in a lifetime. The sea, that old and unyielding companion, has grown restless. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves, with their ceaseless fury, seem to rise in defiance of the very heavens. I fear the storm that now gathers on the horizon may not be one to be weathered by mortal hands. And so, with the last light of the day fading behind the darkening clouds, I must write.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since the last time our paths crossed. You were a child then, still clinging to the notion that love and duty could coexist without rancor. I was a fool, blind to the pain I caused you, and I have spent these years in quiet torment, haunted by the memory of your face—pale, tear-streaked, and so full of sorrow. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, for fear that the sound of it would bring back the tears I have tried to bury.

I have always been a man of duty, Eliza. My life has been the lighthouse, steadfast, unyielding, a beacon for those who sail the dark waters of the world. But duty is a cruel master, and it has cost me more than I ever thought possible. I have

**April 12, 1897**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old tower. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is a bruise of black and green. I have seen storms like this before—many, many times—but tonight feels different. I feel it in my bones. The light has been burning all night, as it must, and I have been watching the waves, the way they crash against the rocks below, as if they were trying to reach me.

I have not seen you in five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I have written to you, I have sent letters, but they have gone unanswered. **Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**  
*Written in the hand of a man who has long since lost the use of his own name, but still remembers the weight of a father’s duty.*

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

Five years have passed since the last time I saw your face. Five years since you walked out of this house, your eyes full of fire and your heart full of something I could not understand. I have not heard from you, nor have I dared to write. But tonight, the storm comes, and I fear I may not survive it. And if I do not, I must say what I have never had the courage to say.

You were a fine girl, Eliza. Bright, spirited, with a mind that could outwit the stars. I watched you grow, and I watched you change. You were not the child I raised, but the woman you became. I tried to guide you, but you would not be guided. You wanted to be your own person, and I… I did not understand that. I thought I was protecting you. I thought I was doing what was right.

But perhaps I was wrong.

I have spent these years alone, with the sea and the wind and the ever-watchful light. I have kept the beacon burning through every storm, every night, every year. I have stood on this rock, watching the world below, and I have wondered if I had done enough. If I had been enough.
